paragraphs.text: Dear Sirs! I would like to draw your attention to a number of issues that I have seen as an internet user and player. First, when entering newspapers’ websites, blogs and the like, it will pop up the data request window. I have been struggling to read the list of “partners” of the website, i.e. companies that download my data, rather than clicking on “accept everything”, just because I want to see an article on the internet. Many of these letters, who are allowed to obtain data, and I remind you, started with I wanted to look at some online edition of a newspaper! – it is very long, complex, manually clicking on ‘I don’t allow’ with sometimes almost three hundred ‘partners’ and takes a long time, so many internet users simply give them their data because they want to access the content of the website. Would it be possible to assume that, if the user does not ‘agree’ immediately (and that this is not the default option on these lists...), he does not agree with the transfer? Apart from the question of ensuring that the ownership of an electronic game is similar to the physical ownership of a copy (in order for EA slicing companies not to be able, if they close the EA platform, to access Sims 4, in which grams), I would be very grateful if something changed, at the same time, how many companies require external applications, providing data, creating accounts, etc. only in order to obtain a service that did not previously require as many of their data to be provided and subscriptions instead of a one-off payment to something that does not require subscription (e.g. language course on the internet). I have heard that, with the introduction of sound rules related to advertising, data, etc., the Commission may be planning to introduce rules similar to the UK Online Safety Act. I hope that you will not follow this route, censorship, influence on external institutions through the law of the country concerned (e.g. Wikipedia!) and impairment of citizens’ ability to use the internet (people and criminals will find ways to circumvent the new safeguards, the most vulnerable will be those who respect the law). I understand the protection of children, but it is unacceptable to limit people’s right to discuss migration, LGBT+ and other sensitive topics online. The EU cannot be blind to these issues, it must not violate the right to privacy by looking at private news, censoring comments and the like. The adhesion of the ‘extreme right’ to the parties, users, etc. will not help to fight disinformation, as nothing helps the case as a feeling of being persecuted and the first ‘martyrs’. If some of the issues in the new law were to do so, I hope this will not happen, because the best way to combat polarisation is to give the voice to those who are not in favour of being able to live alone. Yours sincerely,
